# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Suggest more Mindf*ck movies like 'The Substance'

**Score:** 53
**Body:** Wtf was the substance ending 
It was 1 am , I've just finished watching the substance and honestly i don't know where to begin. I'm finding it hard to describe the feelings. Did i watch the greatest Body Horror  movie or a brialliantly made movie that went absolutely insane in final minutes.

This is a movie that stays with you
Honestly the entire movie was brilliant cinema but don't know why they went batshit crazy in the Final 10 minutes or so. WTF was that ?? It kinda threw me off but I guess they went for the shock value . I immediately googled the director and shocked to see it was a Female that shot those insane objectification scenes but it kinda makes sense too. There were subtle sattires throughout the film dealing with woman objectification, insecurities and ugly truth of how Film circuits operate. All in all. I absolutely loved it and would recommend it to all my friends.
Looking for more such mind bending movie suggestions i might have missed. Movies that playfully use satire to comment on harsh truths of society and moral values and that stay with you and compel you to think . 
I'm not a hardcore horror fan but ok with gore and violence. Some movies that come to top of my head are Interstellar-Top notch Filmmaking and The Platform on Netflix a brilliant satire on Society.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Videodrome

Naked Lunch
2. Beau is Afraid!!!
  - I saw this in cinema and genuinely muttered “what the fuck” under my breath every 5-10 minutes. I loved it but… what the fuck? 😂
3. Titane (2021) 
There are even some body horror elements
4. Jacob's Ladder, Steppenwolf.
5. Sorry to Bother You
6. Donnie Darko

Brazil
7. Killing of a sacred deer. The Lobster. Spun. Requiem for a dream. Pi.
8. Triangle (2009)
9. Coherence

## 2. Strange Children’s Movies

**Score:** 43
**Body:** I’m looking for strange weird cool children’s movies. I love movies like- Marcel the Shell with Shoes On, Secret of Nimh, Stardust… whimsical, fantastical, interesting. Old or new, animation or live action… I want to be whisked into weird worlds and stories. 🥰 Thank you for your time!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Dark Crystal
2. Return to Oz
3. Time Bandits
4. Spirited Away
5. The Neverending Story
6. Flight of the Navigator
7. Labyrinth
8. Pippy Longstocking

Don’t ask about the Peanut Butter Solution
9. The Black Cauldron is an underated Disney movie imo.
10. The Last Unicorn

## 3. Movies with a similar feel to Sicario's tunnel scene?

**Score:** 24
**Body:** I absolutely loved Sicario's tunnel scene. So intense, the music was perfect and kept you at the edge of the seat. I want to feel this again.

  
What other movies can I watch with a similar feel to this? especially with a great soundtrack too

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Alien 1
  - Aliens "inside the room" scene is pretty intense too.
2. 1917. Pretty much the whole movie is this feeling.
3. Zero Dark Thirty 

Munich 

Collateral 

Black Hawk Down 

The Hurt Locker 

Man on Fire 

The Covenant 2023
  - BHD first half of the movie is some bad ass action, then in night it calms down too much

Munich is good as well
  - Those are all good movies, but I don't remember a tense tunnel scene in any of them.
4. *Heat* has a few scenes on or above that level.
5. Civil War. Surprisingly good soundtrack. Genuinely surprised to hear some of the songs in it, coming from a person who listens to a lot of pretty obscure music.

It definitely deserves a higher rating than it has as well.
6. Wind River 
7. The Decent.

## 4. Looking for a movie that will stress me out

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I just finished the movie Saturday Night (10/10 and I will defend that to the death) and it was so fast paced and the whole movie is about meeting the most important deadline of everyone's life and it **stressed me the f out!** and it was incredible.

I'd love to see more of this.  It doesn't have to be fast paced.  I assume the "Quiet" movies will make me feel the same level of anxiety and I'm all for it.  I haven't seen those so please also let me know if I should.

Please, give me your best.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Uncut Gems
  - On that same note, Good Time
2. Uncut Gems. Fast talking anxious people making one bad decision after another.
3. Beau is Afraid
4. Whiplash (2014)
5. 10 Cloverfield Ln
6. Run Lola Run is a fantastic example. Memento, CRANK, Saw 1.


Edit: 8mm with Nic Cage is a absolute favorite of mine and still stresses me everytime I see it. See also Leaving Las Vegas for a stress of knowing this won't end well and you want it to.
7. Sicario
8. Uncut gems. Gave me so much anxiety, just a lot goin on and lots of shouting
9. Uncut Gems

Good Time

Requiem for a Dream

Sicario is pretty stressful too 

And yes, Run Lola Run for sure

## 5. Looking for something similar to (Space Balls) movie. Comedy/parody/sci-fi silly movies

**Score:** 14
**Body:** hello, recently I've seen the movie (Space balls) and it was very nice silly comedy and reminded me of (Police academy) also. I've been looking for similar parody/comedy movies . Do you guys have any suggestions, can be Old or New and no (scary movie parody series please).and I also seen (Naked gun) 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Galaxy Quest
2. Anything by Mel Brooks.  The Adventures of Baron Munchausen.
3. Airplane!, Top Secret!, Robin Hood: Men in Tights, Galaxy Quest, and Hot Shots! (and Part Deux).
  - Adding to this: The Ice Pirates
4. *Austin Powers*.
5. Ice Pirates
6. Space Truckers.

The cast is stacked, the effects are chessy, over all its a lot of fun.
7. Blazing saddles

Three amigos
8. Young Frankenstein sorry fronkenstein
9. Kung Pow ! Enter The Fist

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Why are movies so dark now?

**Score:** 5922
**Body:** I think I haven’t seen any movie premiered after 2020 because of this single reason. They’re not enjoyable anymore. Why is every movie so dark now? Even the unserious ones? Who told them this was the vibe? 2000s movies were so neat and colorful that’s what made me watch them, I could be doing anything else at home and then I’d see for example Click (2003) and it would catch my eye because it was a very colorful movie. Now I have to stay focused so I don’t lose the topic of the movie. There’s no visual pleasure anymore. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There are several reasons but the largest bar none is the move from shooting film to digital. That brings with it some big shifts:
1) film is less forgiving. Meaning it requires more forethought by DPs as opposed to the chuckleheads these days fresh out of film school who shoot everything in focus and flatly lit so they can punt on making decisions or “fix it in post”. 
2) film is softer and analog. Digital is sharper and binary. The reason on set makeup and post beauty work budgets have blown up is not because actors got uglier. The sensors on pro digital cameras are amazing and capture every sin and pore, and every light value hitting the lens, including what is beyond the limits of the human eye. Film is a chemical reaction that I’m not equipped to explain but the captured image is well, narrower and gauzier. 
3) this all leads to the last shift. Color timing (aka DI work) allows the look of a show or film to be dialed up to an 11 or sanded down to a 0. Film color timing was a laborious but Chemical process back in the olden days. See Saving private ryan for a good BTS into that. But then Da Vinci Resolve and all the digital color correction tools came onto the scene, which combined with digital camera footage opened up a whole new world of control over the final image. I mentioned DPs nowadays not knowing what they are doing. Guess what happens when the director or showrunner gets into the bay and doesn’t like what the DP shot? Change it! They now have the ability to alter the look of the whole thing in post without needing to reshoot like you would on film. Will it look good if it was shot with one look in mind and graded for another look? No. But it’s possible. What if a director or creative is high on his own supply and wanted a dark or moody look? Dial it up to 11! That’s when often DPs and directors tell their colorist to dial down the image as dark as possible. Then the producers come in and go “we can’t see the actors faces”. Arguments ensue and you get the results you’re most often complaining about nowadays. 

Your post has images of Devil wears Prada. Here’s what that was shot on according to google:

“The Devil Wears Prada (2006) was primarily shot on 35mm film using Arricam ST cameras and Zeiss Ultra Prime lenses, with some scenes utilizing the Aaton A-Minima. Cinematographer Florian Ballhaus shot the film in the Super 35 format, specifically employing Kodak Vision2 200T 5217 and 500T 5218 stocks”

I guarantee the sequel was shot on either Arri Alexa or a RED in 8k for HDR Dolby. 


Source: I work in DI post. Sorry for the rant.
  - This is why I love Reddit. Some random thing gets posted, and an expert in that subject shows up to explain the whys and wherefores. 

Thanks for taking the time to explain!
    - Very true, until you get someone else that replies with “um ackshually…”
    - And their name is something like grumpleforeskin lol
  - No need to apologize! That was a very informative rant. Thanks for sharing
  - Thanks, this is really informative on a topic I never considered before!
2. 90s and early 2000s are over. The color was sucked out of this world, along with everyone's happiness
3. There's truth to many films being underlit now but it's also worth mentioning a lot of 90s and 2000s studio films like the one you mentioned are overlit. We peaked in the Golden Age/Technicolor era and then again in the 70s and early 80s!

Anyway I would not look at a film by…David Frankel as an example of being visually strong.
4. Because studios film them to be streamed at home rather than theatrical releases. And in a land of umpteen million screens, it's impossible to color or gamma correct for all of them. So they choose one that looks the least offensive to the most people and leave it up to the rest of us to be picky and manually adjust.

Same reason why movie sound has changed so much. They all used to be recorded and edited for surround sound and open spaces, now they're all made for sound bars and consumer speakers.
  - And I still have to turn the movie up and down based on an action scene or a talking scene smh

## 2. This movie dealt with such an important issue, and Mark Ruffalo delivered one of the finest performances in it.[Dark Waters]

**Score:** 486
**Body:** Dark Waters was released in 2019 and was based on a true story.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The film follows real life lawyer Robert Bilott (Mark Ruffalo) as he goes up against chemical giant DuPont for poisoning a community with toxic PFOA.
2. Dark Waters is a great movie.

I highly recommend the documentary The Devil We Know (2018), which is also about DuPont's use of PFAS. It is emotionally taxing, but so important.
3. Kinda throwing scientists under the bus there. I'm personally offended
  - Well did y'all invent plastic, or not? 
    - Plastic is a great invention. It’s overuse everywhere because of how cheap and easy it is is on corporations and societal laziness
      - It should never be used with anything food related. Ever
  - Same. I actually think scientists are doing some of the best work out of everyone to protect people
4. Great movie
5. I threw out all of my pans in the middle of the night.
6. Strangely relevant.

## 3. When it comes to versatility and acting prowess, Hugh Jackman is firmly in the top league.

**Score:** 255
**Body:** Despite playing a beloved superhero for such a long time, he continued to build a career filled with varied and versatile roles.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hard to knock the guy, he delivers !
2. His range and filmography is pretty great, I'd say top tier
3. The Prestige remains my favorite Hugh Jackman movie.
  - Favorite Nolan film for me. Maybe tied with Memento. And I'm not usually a huge Nolan fan
    - Early Nolan is better
4. He's great and. But this picture suggests his real strength is in his hair and beard acting.
5. Shame you left out movie 43
6. There is a sword fish, where he is the ripped hacker.
7. He really is good in every genre of film he is in. His musical based films are 🔥
8. Australia was pretty good too

## 4. Characters who died way too soon ?

**Score:** 137
**Body:** The vanisher in Deadpool 2.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fun fact. If you look closely you can spot Vanisher in every X-men movie up to his death in Deadpool 2.
  - I had a hell of a time finding him in X-3.  Mostly because that movie was unwatchable.
2. MacGruber’s entire team
  - When Kunth tells him he’s assembled a team but accidentally blows them up and Macgrubers like “no way no way” oh man I love that movie
3. Steven Seagal died pretty early in *Executive Decision* especially considering how marketed the film was around the actor yet how minor a role he played with Kurt Russel being the major lead.
4. All the X-force except Domino got killed within five mins
  - Not all.  One of them had very good luck.
5. Bryan Cranston in Godzilla (2014) 

Movie would've been 50x better if it was a father son road movie about trying to stop Kaijus.
6. Roxy in Kingsman: The Golden Circle. What a waste
7. Samuel L Jackson gives a rousing speech to the battered survivors… and then gets promptly grabbed by the shark.

## 5. Re: Why are movies so dark now?

**Score:** 125
**Body:** It’s all choice, taste, and HDR. Modern digital can easily provide for a poppier, brighter image. Things are dark because they’re made that way, most likely in post, complicated by the push for HDR content.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't mind content being dark tbh since movies are mastered for dark rooms

That said, I hate the new trend of HDR movies with highlights at 200 nits. That's just SDR with a wide color gamut at this point
  - It depends. GoT had some scenes that were unwatchable, regardless of how bright the room is. I'm still a big fan of actual lighting.

Watching the DWP2 trailer, I can't / don't really fault any of the editors / colorists / director's choices, I just think tastes have "looks" trending more to the subtle and understated side of things.
    - I don'T disagree. That beach scene in house of the dragon was pure cancer

It was more of a comment in general. 

For games of thrones I don't remember any scenes that made me struggle, though. Only people I know that struggled had low end TVs that crushed shadow details
      - The long night episode in particular are what most people are talking about. I watched it on 100 inch screen and it was pretty bad
2. Movie exposure is not like photography exposure. You cannot take a still frame and edit it like a photo and fill out the histogram. You need to maintain consistent exposure levels across multiple camera angles in the same environment. Another angle might have something much brighter in that same scene, but if you already maxed out the histogram, there will be no dynamic difference. 

You are taking one static moment and judging it rather than evaluating the contrast and exposure of the entire scene.

There are plenty of scenes in the trailer that have brighter exposures and more contrast. 

I can do the same thing with Raiders and claim it is too dark.

https://preview.redd.it/d28llfey2yhg1.jpeg?width=3840&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=7b83697e6c6eb1e2176cddf3f0c67d8cb73ec5ee


  - Also, the exposure level and contrast seem consistent with the original movie when you compare similar compositions and lighting environments.

https://preview.redd.it/q0vt4jon4yhg1.jpeg?width=2436&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=7338440120e419244cad2e235f329be8ab7c3dc9
3. I cant hear shit either. Music is blasting and then they talk and you turn it up, then here comes the music again at 120 db.
4. Dune is my go-to example of this going too far, and I do like Denis Villeneuve, but there's a scene in a dark room with a dark background and a dark chair and both characters are wearing black costumes and I just kinda threw my hands up like, "Why bother filming this at all?"

However, I did feel like darkness and murkiness were used minimally to great effect in Bladerunner 2049.

At home, I definitely crank the brightness up on most modern movies.
  - Remember that one GoT episode that was pitch black for like 90% of the runtime? Producers went on an on about how they spent ages filming this episode and what a huge effort it was, and I'm like "and you wasted millions of dollars and thousands of people's time just to not bother with proper lighting?"
  - This reminds me of movies in which the background music is so loud, you can’t hear the dialogue…

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Melania: knowing the words but not the music

**Score:** 226
**Body:** I recently saw Melania and it may be the longest 100 minute movie I've ever seen. Littered between seemingly endless scenes of Mrs. Trump going about her daily activities is a plethora of licensed music, which undoubtedly attributed to the large budget for what was hardly a big budget production. 

With that said, there were 1 or 2 interesting moments that had actually nothing to do with Mrs. Trump, in one scene the alleged rapist and director of the movie Brett Ratner expresses his disdain for Hollywood reporters which is for some reason left in the movie. However, what this post is actually about is a sequence that occurs towards the end of the film where there is an homage to Goodfellas as we see Mr. and Mrs. Trump make their way through some hallways on their way to an inaugural ball. "Then He Kissed Me" by the The Crystals plays while this occurs, much like the famous Copacabana oner from Goodfellas...except not at all as that scene, besides being one take unlike the quick and cut together sequence in Melania, is showing us how someone who was not raised in the criminal life (Karen) could be swept off her feet and easily fall into it. It's glamourous, overwhelming, beautiful and exciting, it shows us exactly how we, the audience, are Karen in this moment and throughout much of the film. It's a terrific scene in an excellent movie, so much is communicated with practically no dialogue.

Melania attempts to pay homage to this sequence by using the music along with a tracking shot here and there but it's a complete dud because it lacks any meaning to the story being communicated. Melania is already rich and famous and has been first lady once before, as she sassily reminds the audience in the opening sequence (set to another Scorsese favourite, Gimme Shelter). That's why this movie is so emblematic of the Trump movement and its trappings more broadly, it has all the hallmarks of the elite but lacks the elegance, the manners, the traditions that actually shaped the ruling class of old. It gropes at images it cannot understand and thinks that deploying them alone is meaning. It knows the words, but not the music.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >I recently saw Melania

If you are self harming please reach out to a trained crisis counselor. Call 988 to speak to someone. While the people on the hotline are not medical professionals, they are trained in crisis intervention and can help you if you are feeling the desire to harm yourself in the moment. You matter, and we want you here and healthy.

For more information please visit NAMI's excellent resource page: https://helplinefaqs.nami.org/
  - One of the better replies to my lifetime of idiotic behaviour, thank you for the belly laugh.
2. I think there's something to be said about the stark difference between the ascent of working class criminals to a life of luxury and the ascent of pampered, tasteless elites to more and more positions of power. Henry Hill is the fantasy of people I grew up with. You get a decent chunk of cash, you get a good house, a sexy car, go into the back of the fancy restaurants. Buy anything you want. 


What we see in the Trumpian worldview is a gawdy, repugnant lack of decorum. It is the kind of ascent that rips people's lives apart while yelling about the necessity of a new ballroom or an arch to oneself. It is a garish looking boot trying to stamp it's image into the face of the world. Trump has some of the trappings of a working class criminal, with his love of rock music, his appetite for fast food, his filterless bigotry, and his complete lack of understanding with anything decorative. But it never becomes fun, or at least it wears away quickly once you see that there is no real rise. There is no warped hero's journey. Here is a man who was born with a silver spoon and got to keep it. Here is a man that, unlike Henry Hill, did not have to fight for anything at all. He did not earn his super model wife, he bought her, and quite literally it seems. And he didn't succeed in his criminal empire. He failed continously, simply propped up by the same elite shielding that allows few of the rich to truly fail. 


Then I think it's interesting that after all that, Trump is the only monster worth diving into. Melania is just an expressionless prop that stands next to him. A plastic age Eva Braun. The fact that she thought she was interesting enough for a documentary is astounding. 
3. Mark Kermode (who is fair but can be harsh when it's called for) just reviewed Melania, and here is a [link to his scathing review](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DTyUni8dbkE). Near the end of the review (at 7:55), he notes some of the inscrutable/ironic music choices. Most strikingly, Gimme Shelter: *Rape, murder, it's just a shot away*. Like... could the timing be worse? Via the Epstein files (latest bolus with the same release date as Melania), Trump is implicated in rape & murder (threat/accessory), and via Trump, multiple citizens have been shot/murdered by ICE in the last month.
  - So many awesome sound bites in that review! 

Paraphrasing : 


"It's the most depressing thing I've ever watched at the cinema... And I've watched Cannibal Holocaust and A Serbian Film"

"It's not a movie, it's a propaganda piece and a paid for bribe" 

"It's like Eva Braun making a documentary about how she's sad about war while watching Hitler invade Poland" 

"Derek Zoolander has more self awareness and is more self deprecating"
4. The ironic thing about the Melania doc is that there is a fascinating documentary to be made about this woman and we got this dud. Why not explore what her life was in whatever Eastern European village she came from? Her pornographic modeling career? Her connections to Russia? Her connections to Epstein? Her life as a high end escort working shady NY night clubs exclusive to rich men? Her obtaining a genius visa despite having no academic accomplishments of note and no college education? Her weird relationship with Donald Trump, where they live separate lives and yet pretend to be a couple? From the reviews I've read, none of these topics are covered in the doc. Not that I expected that from a propaganda piece directed by Brett Ratner, but it is worth pointing out how hard this fails as a credible documentary film.
5. Maybe instead of elite, it's actually alluding to Melania/Trump being part of a criminal enterprise like Goodfellas. Trump is a criminal, and Melania can't feign ignorance.  Karen/Melania don't care where the money comes from.  
  - I don't think Ratner is sophisticated enough to pull off that kind of nuance.
    - Nah, I think they think mobsters are people to be admired and don't understand why that's bad.
      - Correct, they are the crowd at the end of The Wolf of Wall Street.

## 2. They Shoot Pictures, Don't They updated 21st century film rankings

**Score:** 18
**Body:** [TSPDT - The 21st Century's 1000 Most Acclaimed Films (Table)](https://www.theyshootpictures.com/21stcentury_allfilms_table.php)

The current top ten:

|Pos|2025|Title|Director|Year|Country|Mins|
|:-|:-|:-|:-|:-|:-|:-|
|1|1|In the Mood for Love|Wong Kar-wai|2000|Hong Kong|97|
|2|2|Mulholland Dr.|Lynch, David|2001|USA|147|
|3|3|Yi Yi|Yang, Edward|2000|Taiwan|173|
|4|4|Spirited Away|Miyazaki, Hayao|2001|Japan|124|
|5|5|There Will Be Blood|Anderson, Paul Thomas|2007|USA|158|
|6|6|Tropical Malady|Weerasethakul, Apichatpong|2004|Thailand|118|
|7|7|Portrait of a Lady on Fire|Sciamma, Céline|2019|France|121|
|8|8|Tree of Life, The|Malick, Terrence|2011|USA|139|
|9|9|Gleaners & I, The|Varda, Agnès|2000|France|82|
|10|10|Moonlight|Jenkins, Barry|2016|USA|111|

Any thoughts on this list or TSPDT in general?

My biggest critique, personally, is that a lot of fantastic 21st century documentaries that I love either aren't on the list or are relatively low. 

I also think that TSPDT could be more transparent about their methodology in general, but that's not specific to this list. 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I only recently watched Moonlight. I just felt like I didn’t have too much in common with the demographics and subject matter of the film. But that was an incredible movie. Highly recommend. I wouldn’t necessarily put it in the top 10, but definitely deserves to be in the top 50 at least.  
  - I had the same experience. I put it off for a long time, thinking that it probably wouldn’t resonate with me. I couldn’t believe how wrong I was. That was one of the most brilliant and well-composed films I’ve ever seen. It was an absolute masterpiece, down to the very last shot.
2. I like these lists, they're useful for finding stuff to watch, but it's funny to notice some anomalies that come about. 

*Moulin Rouge in the top 100 and Chicago out of the top 1000

*Almost all of Kelly Reichardt's films in the top 200, Kore-eda's Nobody Knows behind in top 400.

*Somehow Kiyoshi Kurosawa's Cloud is in but After Yang is out.

*Also Juror#2 being in there at all haha

I'm guessing the difference between the latter half and the films outside the list might not be that meaningful in terms of how they polled it, but I don't know.

## 3. A question about Bela Tarr

**Score:** 9
**Body:** Some years ago, I worked my way through Tarkovsky's films and loved every minute of them. In an attempt to try and find something slightly similar in terms of pacing and mood, I discovered Bela Tarr. I read that Werkmeister Harmonies is a good entry point, so I decided to start there. I watched the film and just couldn't connect with it all, and assumed Bela Tarr was not for me.
A few years later, I decided to give his work another try and jumped in the deep end with Satantango and I absolutely loved it!
Figuring that I may have watched Werkmeister Harmonies at the wrong time, I decided to give it a second shot. To my surprise, I am still completely indifferent to the film. I am interested in checking out his other films like Damnation, The Man From London and Turin Horse, but I was wondering how these films fair when compared to Werkmeister Harmonies and Satantango. I mean, I know that none of them are 7 hours long, but I would like to know where they stand in terms of quality. Do some people prefer Damnation or these other films? Did anyone else struggle with Werkmeister Harmonies? Curious to learn more about other people's viewing experience

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Although it might be somewhat inane to talk about ratings, I rate Werckmeister Harmonies in fourth place of the five black and white Bela Tarr feature films, with The Turin Horse fifth.

To make this comment an adequate length, I’ll add that there was a [link](https://apollo-magazine.com/bela-tarr-obituary-peter-strickland-satantango-hungary/) to a nice article about Tarr by the filmmaker Peter Strickland linked in another post about Tarr last month. On the notion of the Tarkovsky-Tarr similarity — you say you’re coming as a fan of Tarkovsky to Tarr — Strickland writes:

>Tarr is often compared with Andrei Tarkovsky but although their films share a meditative dimension, Tarr’s work is bereft of Tarkovsky’s ecclesiastical leanings. In Tarr’s world, church bells might ring, but God doesn’t want to know. Narcissists and demagogues have taken God’s place, and this, beyond the textural and structural thrills that continue to wow audiences, is what roots Tarr’s work in the real world.
2. I think Damnation is very good, you'll find things to like in that movie. Turin Horse is the absolute purest distillation of what Tarr wanted to make movies for. I haven't seen Sátántangó yet, I'm sure it is more layered. Turin Horse is probably more singularly focused, but it does a great job at it.
3. I’m not sure if this is super relevant to the conversation, but regarding Satantango and WM, I personally found Satantango to be more compelling as a film, and  (as Melancholy of Resistance) to be more compelling as a book. I think this is because where WM is very much a distillation of MR, which focuses on the main character, rather than fleshing out the rest of the text, I find that Tarr’s rendition of Satantango builds upon and expands Krasnahorkai’s text. I very much enjoy both films, but I find that Satantango really transforms the book in such a way that almost supplants it. This is, in part, due to the run time (you could arguably read Satantango faster than it takes to watch it). 
4. Like you, I am a big Tarkovsky fan, and while I also enjoyed Tarr's movie to some extent, I wouldn't call them particularly similar. Out of the three that I watched (Satantango, Werckmeister Harmonies, and Turin Horse), Turin Horse is the one that stood out the most to me - maybe because I had already read the novels the other two were based on, and felt like the movies didn't add anything meaningful to what was already in the books (in the case of Werckmeister Harmonies, it actually removed some parts from the source material, if I remember correctly). But yeah, I would definitely agree that Werckmeister Harmonies is the weakest out of the three that I saw, both visually and in terms of meaning (the book it is based on, however, is outstanding).
5. Are you me??? wtf??? I am literally in the exact same boat except the first time I tried Werckmeister Harmonies, I got through half of it and honestly just couldn’t keep going. I just tried seeing it again a few days ago and had the same experience. 

The first 15 minutes or so are fantastic but everything after that is so dull. The visuals are fantastic but it’s really missing that emotional component. There’s nothing to really latch onto either.

6. This is all subjective, but I think Satantango is the best of all his films, though I really love The Turin Horse and Werkmeister Harmonies as well. I remember not liking The Man From London as much, but haven't seen it in years. Satantango has a looser, less formal feeling than his latter films, that's why I prefer to revisit it. It's also damn funny. 

By the way, watch the documentary I Used To Be a Filmmaker, if you haven't already. It really gives a glimpse into the people who made these films.
7. I'm a huge fan of Tarr's and Satantango is my favorite of his. I love his other films, but some don't grasp me the way others do. I've watched Satantango four times since first seeing it in 2024 and it just keeps me enraptured.

My ranking for his black and white films goes  
1) Satantango  
2) The Turin Horse  
3) Damnation  
4) The Werckmeister Harmonies  
5) The Man From London
  - Thanks. This encourages me to not let my experience of Werckmeister Harmonies hold me back from his other films
8. It depends on what you liked about Satantango or didn’t like about WH - “connected with” and “quality” are pretty vague. I would say that he has kinda three semi-distinct periods in his filmography; the last third, the longest one, for which he is famous, is stylistically, and in terms of its anthropological worldview, very homogeneous. In terms of story, Satantango is the most rounded out, then probably The Man from London, which is a very simple crime story… those are the ones I found the most compelling to watch, but I like rounded-out stories. I barely remember Damnation, and The Turin Horse is a slog with lots of repetition, comically so - on purpose, I assume - but it does not make a compelling viewing experience, in my opinion. I really find that, other than the meditative style, he is not very similar to Tarkovsky. Tarkovsky is waaay more spiritual, and that makes his movies more comfortable to be in. Tarr was an atheist, and he has a lot of empathy for humans, and one reason you might have connected more easily with Satantango is because that’s the one of his films where that empathy is more obvious and comes through the most sentimentally, but other than that, his movies happen in a cold, indifferent world, which might be off-putting. I don't know if it helps...

## 4. What should I screen for my 30th birthday?

**Score:** 7
**Body:** Hi!

I'm turning 30 in April and have the chance to screen something in an independent cinema in a historical building in my city. It has a little cafe where we will have some wine before and after. I love film and this will be the perfect celebration.

But... what to watch? I'm looking for something visually stunning, meaningful, powerful, possibly funny and maybe niche, just to decrease the change of people having seen it before. Ideally, it should be either female directed/produced or at least have female main characters that are not portrayed through the male gaze. 

I'm considering Orlando (1992, my favourite film of all time), though it might be a little slow/serious. Or Paprika? Or Daisies (1966). I was also considering RRR but it's definitely too long. 

I'm also happy to go in the documentary direction.

  
I want people to leave happy, inspired, mind blown, maybe a little provoked? We're gonna have a party afterwards to I hope to set the right mood - I'd love it if it felt celebratory or just a good conversation starter.

  
I feel like I'm asking too much, but then there are so many beautiful movies out there!

  
Thank you, Reddit!

  


### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Cleo From 5-7" by Agnes Varda gets my vote.

1962 French film that follows a young singer through a day in Paris, while she's waiting for some important news.  It's light and breezy, beautifully shot, with depth and meaning that sneaks up and stays with you. You mentioned not wanting movies portraying female characters through the male gaze.  You could think of Cleo" as a movie with a female gaze, interested in how the female character sees herself.

## 5. Taste Of Fear (1961) - Hitchcock level suspense!

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I wish they still made them like this.  
  
A simple setup with a great protagonist who draws you in immediately. I can’t believe I’ve never encountered Susan Strasberg (daughter of legendary acting coach Lee) before - she’s spellbindingly beautiful and her subtly reactive face is like a thousand special effects for the whole runtime (a tight 82 minutes).  
  
She returns to her family mansion following a tragedy, eager to see her dad. She’s bound to a wheelchair and might be going crazy as she sees what appears to be her absent father around the gothic building. The atmosphere and suspense keep your mind spinning, it’s all so *engaging* and makes modern films seem like porn by comparison.  
  
Ronald Lewis is another revelation. He plays a mild mannered chauffeur who provides the only comfort to Penny as her paranoia about her sugary stepmother and her creepy friend the doctor (played by Christopher Lee) multiplies.  
  
The black and white noir photography by Douglas ‘Indiana Jones’ Slocombe is gorgeous and haunting, and director Seth Holt knows just where to place the camera to draw you in, and just when to unleash the next revelation in the story. The manipulation of the viewer would impress Hitch himself.  
  
The mystery is always compelling but the final reveal will have you smacking your gob so hard you’ll somersault.  
  
**This** is filmmaking. One day I hope it comes back.

2. I watched this film a couple of months ago and I was completely blown away. The film felt very Hitchcockian and while I believed Strasberg's character was honest in her assumption of seeing an apparition, I was also prepared in case it was revealed to be a red herring. That said, the build up in psychological mystery and suspense was compelling, but the plot twist was more than I expected. 

Susan Strasberg was the daughter of famed acting coaches Lee and Paula Strasberg. She never quite reached super stardom, though I enjoyed in other films like *Picnic*, *Kapò*, and her last film *The Other Side of the Wind* (which was completed decades after her death) where she plays an unveiled take of Pauline Kael. She was amazing in this film.
